All the people, the teachers in New York were not that they were concerned, I guess, but they didn't appear.

But my feeling was that I was entitled to use that pool that I was my right to use it.
If I had been white, that time, even if I couldn't swim was not the issue.
They would be teaching me to swim. They were teaching white kids who couldn't swim.
That was the time they were taught them to swim.
It was a very stressful time for me.
The result was that instead of allowing everybody to use the pool and opening it, they closed it.
The blood was a bad feature for the school. Each time I had one of the best swimming teams in the city.
I was winning a lot of tournaments and stuff like that.
But it decided that instead of opening the pool, everybody that would close it.
That showed me the great cost to white of segregation. They paid a price for it.
